claim: The official WebAssembly FAQ states: 'Is WebAssembly trying to replace JavaScript? No! WebAssembly is designed to be a complement to, not replacement of, JavaScript.'
The FAQ states: 'JavaScript has an incredible amount of momentum and will remain the single, privileged dynamic language of the Web.'

The FAQ describes three configurations where JS and WASM work together: (1) compiled C++ apps with JavaScript as glue; (2) HTML/CSS/JS UI around a WASM-controlled canvas; (3) mostly HTML/CSS/JS apps with high-performance WASM modules for tasks like graphing, simulation, video processing.
WebAssembly is designed to be implemented inside existing JavaScript engines, reusing the JS engine's compiler backend, ES6 module loading, and security sandboxing.

Official WebAssembly use cases include: image/video editing, CAD applications, AAA games, scientific visualization, VR/AR, encryption, remote desktop, VPN, server-side applications, language interpreters, and developer tooling.
A listed use case is 'Main frame in WebAssembly, but the UI is in JavaScript/HTML' — confirming WASM does not replace the UI layer.
Figma published that 'WebAssembly cut Figma's load time by 3x' and their core rendering engine is powered by WebAssembly compiled from C++.
Cloudflare Workers supports running Rust, Zig, Python (via Pyodide), and Haskell compiled to WebAssembly in production at the edge.
Google V8 actively develops WASM features including: JSPI (JavaScript Promise Integration API), GC support for garbage-collected languages, dynamic tiering, tail calls, and up to 4GB WASM memory.
WASM GC allows garbage-collected languages (like Java, Kotlin, Dart) to be compiled efficiently to WebAssembly, sharing objects with JavaScript.

WebAssembly is described as 'a binary instruction format for a stack-based virtual machine, designed as a portable compilation target for programming languages, enabling deployment on the web for client and server applications.'
WASM binary format can be natively decoded more than 20x faster than JavaScript can be parsed, providing critical cold-load performance on mobile.
